questions.stem: In an interview, what problem does the Singleton pattern primarily address?
options: Ensure a class has one shared instance and a global access point
Clone objects without knowing their concrete classes at compile time
Compose tree structures of part-whole hierarchies for uniform treatment
Decouple an abstraction from its implementation so both can vary
questions.explanation: Singleton restricts instantiation so one shared instance serves the whole process.

questions.stem: Factory Method versus Abstract Factory — which distinction is interview-correct?
options: They are identical GoF aliases with no practical difference
Factory Method creates one product via subclass hooks; Abstract Factory families of related products
Abstract Factory only works for immutable value objects
Factory Method always returns singletons; Abstract Factory never does
questions.explanation: Factory Method: one product type via inheritance. Abstract Factory: product families via composition.

questions.stem: When would you prefer Builder over a telescoping constructor in an interview answer?
options: When you must guarantee exactly one instance across JVM processes
When every field is required and order never varies
When objects need many optional fields assembled step-by-step with a fluent API
When inheritance alone can express all construction variants
questions.explanation: Builder shines for complex, optional, ordered construction without constructor explosion.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Prototype pattern — what is the core idea you should state crisply? Which answer is best?
options: Wrap a class to add behavior without modifying its source
Route requests along a chain until one handler processes them
Capture and restore an object's internal state for undo
Create new objects by cloning an existing prototype instead of subclassing factories
questions.explanation: Prototype clones configured instances when construction is costly or subclasses proliferate.

questions.stem: Adapter pattern — which interview line best describes its intent?
options: Convert one interface into another clients already expect without changing either side
Share fine-grained immutable state across many similar objects to save memory
Provide a simplified entry point over a complex subsystem of classes
Let subclasses redefine steps of an algorithm skeleton
questions.explanation: Adapter translates incompatible interfaces so existing clients can reuse new code.

questions.stem: Decorator versus Inheritance for extending behavior — what's the better interview take?
options: Decorator always replaces the original class definition at compile time
Decorator composes wrappers at runtime; inheritance bakes extensions into a fixed hierarchy
Inheritance is preferred when you need unlimited open-ended combinations
Decorator and inheritance are interchangeable with identical trade-offs
questions.explanation: Decorators stack behaviors dynamically; deep inheritance hierarchies get brittle.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Facade pattern — what problem does it solve in real systems? Which answer is best?
options: Ensure only one thread can enter a critical section at a time
Map relational rows to domain objects automatically
Offer a coarse-grained simple API over a noisy multi-class subsystem
Serialize objects across network boundaries with schema evolution
questions.explanation: Facade reduces coupling by hiding subsystem complexity behind one entry point.

questions.stem: Proxy pattern — name a common production use you'd cite in interviews
options: Building immutable collections with structural sharing only
Generating SQL from LINQ expressions at query time
Compiling regexes ahead of time into finite automata
Lazy loading, access control, caching, or remote stubs that stand in for the real object
questions.explanation: Proxies intercept calls for cross-cutting concerns while preserving the subject's interface.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bridge pattern intent — pick the most accurate statement? Which answer is best?
options: Decouple abstraction from implementation so each hierarchy can evolve independently
Merge two class hierarchies into one via multiple inheritance
Replace switch statements with polymorphic method dispatch only
Guarantee thread-safe publication of shared mutable state
questions.explanation: Bridge separates what clients use from how it is implemented across platforms.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Composite pattern fits which structure best? Which answer is best?
options: Flat key-value caches with eviction policies only
Tree-like part-whole hierarchies where clients treat leaves and composites uniformly
Linear pipelines of pure functions with no nesting
Peer-to-peer mesh networks without hierarchical ownership
questions.explanation: Composite lets you nest components and invoke the same operations on nodes and leaves.

questions.stem: Flyweight — when is it the right interview recommendation?
options: You need exactly one mutable global configuration object
Clients must undo a sequence of document edits
Huge numbers of similar objects share intrinsic state; extrinsic state is passed in
You want to version HTTP APIs with content negotiation
questions.explanation: Flyweight trades per-instance memory for shared intrinsic data plus context parameters.

questions.stem: Strategy pattern — what does it buy you compared to hard-coded conditionals?
options: Force every algorithm into a single inheritance tree forever
Eliminate the need for unit tests around business rules
Automatically persist algorithm choice to a relational database
Swap interchangeable algorithms behind a common interface without changing callers
questions.explanation: Strategy encapsulates families of algorithms and selects them at runtime.

questions.stem: Observer / pub-sub — classic interview definition?
options: Subjects notify dependents of state changes so observers stay loosely coupled
A single handler processes every event in a strict FIFO queue only
Callers poll a database every millisecond for updates
Events are always stored in the same process heap as listeners
questions.explanation: Observer defines a one-to-many dependency for change notification without tight coupling.

questions.stem: Command pattern — which benefit is most interview-relevant?
options: Replace all HTTP verbs with a single RPC endpoint name
Encapsulate requests as objects enabling queueing, logging, and undo/redo
Guarantee ACID across distributed microservices by default
Compile domain models directly into SQL DDL scripts
questions.explanation: Commands reify operations so they can be stored, delayed, composed, or reversed.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Template Method versus Strategy — how do you distinguish them? Which answer is best?
options: Template Method is only for UI; Strategy is only for networking
They are forbidden together in the same codebase by GoF rules
Template Method uses inheritance for algorithm skeleton; Strategy uses composition of behaviors
Strategy requires abstract classes; Template Method requires only interfaces
questions.explanation: Template Method fixes steps in a base class; Strategy plugs whole algorithms as objects.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Chain of Responsibility — best one-line intent? Which answer is best?
options: Ensure every handler must mutate the request before forwarding
Require a central registry that dispatches by numeric priority only
Merge all handlers into one god class for performance
Pass a request along handlers until one (or more) can handle it
questions.explanation: Chain decouples sender from receiver by giving multiple objects a chance to handle.

questions.stem: State pattern — when should you reach for it in an interview?
options: Object behavior changes dramatically with internal state; replace big conditionals
You only need a Boolean flag that never grows new states
All transitions are illegal and must throw immediately
State machines cannot be modeled with objects at all
questions.explanation: State encapsulates per-state behavior and transitions as separate classes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Mediator pattern primary goal? Which answer is best?
options: Expose every colleague's private fields to every other colleague
Centralize complex colleague interactions so objects don't reference each other densely
Replace databases with in-memory maps for all collaboration
Force synchronous RPC between every pair of services
questions.explanation: Mediator reduces chaotic many-to-many links by routing communication through one hub.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Memento — classic use case to mention? Which answer is best?
options: Compress images with lossy codecs before upload
Shard databases by geographic region automatically
Capture and restore object state for undo without violating encapsulation
Translate REST payloads into GraphQL schemas
questions.explanation: Memento stores opaque snapshots so originators can roll back safely.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Visitor pattern — trade-off you should acknowledge? Which answer is best?
options: Add new element types easily; adding new operations is always trivial too
It eliminates the need for double dispatch in every language
Visitors must never traverse composite trees
Add new operations easily across a fixed object structure; adding new element types is hard
questions.explanation: Visitor shines when the element hierarchy is stable but operations keep growing.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Dependency Inversion often pairs with which pattern approach? Which answer is best?
options: Depend on abstractions (interfaces) injected at runtime — Strategy/Factory style seams
Hard-code concrete classes in every call site for clarity
Use only static utility methods with no interfaces
Ban constructors and create objects via reflection only
questions.explanation: DIP + factories/DI keep high-level modules free of low-level concretes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Open/Closed Principle maps cleanly to which GoF idea? Which answer is best?
options: Seal every class so nothing can ever be extended
Extend behavior via new Strategy/Decorator implementations without editing callers
Copy-paste modules whenever requirements change
Prefer global mutable registries over polymorphism
questions.explanation: OCP: open for extension, closed for modification — patterns enable plug-in behavior.

questions.stem: Interview trap: Singleton as a global — what's the honest downside?
options: It always improves horizontal scalability across machines
Singletons cannot hold configuration in any language
Hidden dependencies, hard testing, and accidental shared mutable state
The JVM forbids more than one classloader ever
questions.explanation: Singletons couple callers to a global lifetime and complicate isolation in tests.

questions.stem: Which pattern best models a plugin system for interchangeable payment gateways?
options: Memento storing credit card numbers in memory
Flyweight sharing PAN digits across tenants
Iterator walking every gateway on each charge
Strategy (or Factory Method) selected by config or DI at startup
questions.explanation: Payment providers are classic Strategy/Factory plug-ins behind one interface.

questions.stem: Logging, metrics, and auth checks around a service call — which pattern fits?
options: Decorator or Proxy wrapping the core service while preserving its interface
Prototype cloning log files into the response body
Composite nesting HTTP status codes as tree nodes
Builder assembling stack traces into a fluent UI
questions.explanation: Cross-cutting wrappers are textbook Decorator/Proxy without changing the core.

questions.stem: Null Object pattern — interview value proposition?
options: Replace every Optional with raw null references
Provide a do-nothing implementation so callers avoid null checks
Force NPEs early to surface bugs in production only
Serialize nulls as the string literal "null" always
questions.explanation: Null Object is a safe default collaborator that no-ops instead of being null.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Object pool vs Flyweight — how do they differ? Which answer is best?
options: They are the same pattern under two GoF names
Pools only apply to strings; Flyweights only to threads
Pools reuse expensive mutable instances; Flyweights share immutable intrinsic state
Flyweights always allocate fresh objects; pools never recycle
questions.explanation: Pooling manages lifecycle/reuse; Flyweight minimizes memory via shared data.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Repository pattern in domain layers — what does it abstract? Which answer is best?
options: HTTP routing tables for every microservice endpoint
CPU scheduling queues inside the operating system
CSS layout engines for responsive design
Persistence access behind a collection-like interface over aggregates
questions.explanation: Repositories hide storage details so domain code talks in aggregates, not SQL.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Unit of Work — complementary idea to Repository? Which answer is best?
options: Track changes across a business transaction and commit or roll back together
Spawn one database connection per field write immediately
Disable transactions to maximize write throughput always
Store every domain event only in the browser localStorage
questions.explanation: Unit of Work coordinates dirty tracking and atomic flush of related changes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: CQRS often pairs with which separation? Which answer is best?
options: One shared mutable DTO for all reads and writes forever
Distinct models/handlers for commands (writes) versus queries (reads)
Banning eventual consistency in every distributed system
Replacing databases with CSV files for auditability
questions.explanation: CQRS splits write and read paths when their shapes and SLAs diverge.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Anti-pattern: God Object — what smell are you naming? Which answer is best?
options: A class that only holds immutable data with no behavior
A module that is too small to be useful in isolation
One class knows and does far too much, accumulating unrelated responsibilities
An interface with exactly one implementing class
questions.explanation: God objects violate SRP and become change magnets and bug nests.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When is Factory preferable to exposing constructors publicly? Which answer is best?
options: The type has zero fields and a trivial no-arg constructor only
You want callers to new up every dependency graph manually
Reflection is banned and DI containers are unavailable forever
Construction is complex, varies by type, or must hide concrete classes from clients
questions.explanation: Factories encapsulate creation decisions and keep clients on abstractions.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Double-checked locking Singleton — what must you mention for Java/C#? Which answer is best?
options: Needs volatile/correct memory barriers or you risk seeing a partially constructed instance
It is always safe without any synchronization on modern CPUs
It only works when the singleton holds no fields at all
Language runtimes automatically rewrite it to enum singletons
questions.explanation: Without proper publication guarantees, DCL can expose uninitialized state.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Iterator pattern purpose in collections APIs? Which answer is best?
options: Sort every collection in place before any traversal
Provide sequential access to elements without exposing underlying representation
Guarantee O(1) random access for linked structures
Replace for-each loops with recursive tree walks only
questions.explanation: Iterators abstract traversal so clients don't depend on array vs list internals.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Interpreter pattern — typical niche use? Which answer is best?
options: JIT-compile all JVM bytecode on every method entry
Translate REST to SOAP with zero configuration
Represent and evaluate a simple language/grammar as an object structure
Replace regex engines entirely in every codebase
questions.explanation: Interpreter builds an AST of grammar rules for DSLs and expression evaluation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Which pattern helps undo a sequence of document edits? Which answer is best?
options: Facade that deletes the undo stack on every keystroke
Adapter converting keyboards into mouse events
Flyweight sharing keystroke objects across users only
Command (with mementos or inverse commands) for reversible operations
questions.explanation: Commands (often with Memento) are the classic undo/redo mechanism.

questions.stem: MVC / MVP — which statement is interview-safe?
options: Separate model state from presentation and input handling to reduce UI coupling
Put SQL queries directly inside view templates for speed
Make the view own the database connection pool
Eliminate the model layer in all web applications
questions.explanation: UI architectures isolate domain data from rendering and user gestures.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Event Sourcing stores what as the source of truth? Which answer is best?
options: Only the latest row snapshot with no history retained
A sequence of immutable domain events that can rebuild state by replay
Binary memory dumps of the JVM heap each minute
Normalized third-normal-form tables without append logs
questions.explanation: Event sourcing appends facts; current state is a projection of the stream.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Specification pattern — useful for what? Which answer is best?
options: Generating OpenAPI specs from handwritten YAML only
Enforcing that every class has a public no-arg constructor
Composable business rules expressed as objects that can combine with and/or/not
Validating TLS certificates inside the browser exclusively
questions.explanation: Specifications encapsulate reusable predicates for filtering and validation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Prefer composition over inheritance — which pattern mindset matches? Which answer is best?
options: Always subclass Object for every new behavior variant
Multiple inheritance is required for every extension point
Copy base class source into each subclass file manually
Strategy, Decorator, and Bridge favor has-a collaboration over deep is-a trees
questions.explanation: Modern design favors plugging behaviors via composition, not tall hierarchies.

questions.stem: Interview: pick a pattern for rate-limiting wrappers around an HTTP client
options: Decorator or Proxy that counts calls and rejects excess before delegating
Prototype that clones the entire TCP stack per request
Composite that nests status codes as filesystem paths
Memento that stores every response body forever
questions.explanation: Rate limits are cross-cutting — wrap the client without changing call sites.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why might Abstract Factory appear in UI toolkits? Which answer is best?
options: Ensure only one JFrame exists in the entire operating system
Create families of related widgets (button/scrollbar) matching a look-and-feel
Serialize Swing components into JSON automatically
Replace the event loop with busy-wait polling
questions.explanation: Abstract Factory supplies consistent product families across themes/platforms.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Liskov Substitution — how does it constrain pattern usage? Which answer is best?
options: Any override may weaken preconditions freely for flexibility
Interfaces may throw undocumented errors for every method
Subtype Strategy/Decorator implementations must honor the base contract callers rely on
Substituting types is never allowed once a Factory exists
questions.explanation: LSP: clients must be able to use substitutes without breaking expectations.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Circuit breaker often implemented as which structural idea? Which answer is best?
options: Flyweight sharing open sockets across all tenants unsafely
Builder constructing stack traces into HTML emails
Iterator scanning every microservice on each user click
Proxy/Decorator that short-circuits calls when failure thresholds trip
questions.explanation: Circuit breakers wrap remotes and fail fast while the circuit is open.

questions.stem: Publish-subscribe vs Observer — nuanced interview point?
options: Pub-sub often adds a broker/topic decoupling publishers from subscribers further
They are always identical with the same coupling characteristics
Observer requires a message bus; pub-sub forbids one
Pub-sub only works inside a single threaded process
questions.explanation: Classic Observer is often in-process; pub-sub scales with brokers and topics.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When is Visitor a poor fit? Which answer is best?
options: You only need to add operations and the structure is frozen forever
The element type hierarchy changes often, forcing visitor interface churn
You want double dispatch over a stable set of node types
Compilers need to walk ASTs with many analysis passes
questions.explanation: Unstable element hierarchies make Visitor's dual hierarchy painful.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Smart Proxy for lazy ORM associations — what does it do? Which answer is best?
options: Eager-load the entire database on application startup
Delete orphan rows whenever any field is read
Defer loading related rows until the property is first accessed
Replace foreign keys with natural language descriptions
questions.explanation: Lazy-loading proxies fetch related data on demand transparently.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Builder vs Factory Method — quick contrast? Which answer is best?
options: Builder always returns interfaces; Factory Method always returns primitives
Factory Method cannot return objects in statically typed languages
Builder requires a multithreaded runtime to function
Builder focuses on step-wise construction of one complex product; Factory Method on which subtype to instantiate
questions.explanation: Different axes: construction steps versus polymorphic product choice.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Active Record vs Data Mapper — pattern-level difference? Which answer is best?
options: Active Record binds domain object to persistence; Data Mapper keeps domain free of storage
They are synonyms in every ORM documentation
Data Mapper forbids SQL; Active Record forbids objects
Active Record only works with document databases
questions.explanation: Data Mapper (and Repository) keep domain models persistence-ignorant.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Hexagonal / ports-and-adapters aligns with which GoF mindset? Which answer is best?
options: Put framework annotations deep inside pure domain entities always
Depend on port interfaces; drive adapters (DB, HTTP) via Strategy-like plugging
Call JDBC drivers directly from every UI button handler
Ban interfaces so adapters can never be swapped
questions.explanation: Ports are abstractions; adapters are interchangeable implementations at the edges.
